title: Mark 5
subtitle: Three Restorations: Demoniac, Bleeding Woman, Jairus's Daughter

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Baptism of Jesus

sections.header: Verses 1–20 — The Gerasene Demoniac: Legion into the Pigs

heb.paragraph: My name is Legion — a Roman military unit of 3,000–6,000 soldiers. The name is both descriptive (many demons) and politically charged: Rome’s military power and demonic oppression use the same vocabulary. The exorcism in Gentile territory that destroys the pigs reads, for Mark’s Roman audience, as a cosmic challenge to Roman imperial power.

places.name: Region of the Gerasenes
places.coords: Eastern shore of the Sea of Galilee; Decapolis territory
places.text: This is Gentile land — the pigs confirm it. The Decapolis (ten Greek cities) was a region of Hellenistic culture east of the Jordan. Jesus’s first extended Gentile engagement happens at the far edge of Jewish geography, in a cemetery, among unclean animals.

places.name: The Sea / Steep Bank
places.text: The herd of 2,000 pigs rushing down the steep bank into the sea dramatizes the exorcism’s scale and irrevocability. The sea, which Jesus crossed in the storm, now becomes the grave of Legion.

refs.note: People who sit among the graves and spend their nights keeping secret vigil — the tomb-dweller is an OT figure of uncleanness and idolatry. Jesus enters this extreme uncleanness without contamination.
The God who judges to the third and fourth generation — the demonic claim over territory (v.10) reflects the ancient notion of territorial spirits.
The parallel account confirms the Decapolis mission and adds that the man proclaimed what God had done — equating Jesus with God.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "Legion, for we are many" — the Roman military legion (3,000-6,000 troops) was the symbol of Roman imperial violence in the occupied provinces. Marcus's influential reading sees the exorcism as a covert anti-imperial story: the "Legion" driven into pigs (unclean animals, associated with Rome) and drowned in the sea reverses the Exodus — this time Roman military power is swallowed by the sea. The story's original audience in Roman Galilee would have heard the political resonance.

Marcus: "Go home to your own people and tell them how much the Lord has done for you" — the first explicit evangelistic commission in Mark, given to a Gentile, in Gentile territory. The Decapolis mission anticipates the Great Commission. That the command breaks the Messianic Secret (operative in Jewish territory) and is given in Gentile space suggests that the Secret's function is not about Christology per se but about the management of Jewish messianic expectations.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The Gerasene demoniac is the most fully characterized minor figure in Mark. He has a habitat (tombs), a condition (superhuman strength, self-harm, cries), a name (Legion), a location-preference (v.10: begging not to be sent out of the area), a request (the pigs), and a post-healing description (clothed, in his right mind). The detailed characterization invites reader sympathy before the miracle and makes the village's rejection of Jesus after the healing — preferring the loss of their pigs — all the more striking.

Rhoads: "Sitting, clothed, and in his right mind" — the three details reverse the three elements of the possessed state (roaming naked among tombs, breaking chains, crying out). Rhoads's narrative criticism highlights how Mark uses this reversal-pattern to signal restoration: the character's new state is described by the exact negation of their former state. The village's fear in the face of this restoration is one of Mark's sharpest ironies.

Origen devotes extended attention to the Gerasene demoniac in the Catena tradition. The possessed man living among the tombs is, for him, the image of a soul in complete disorder: its natural home (the body) turned to a place of death. The demons' request to enter the pigs is permitted, says Chrysostom, both to demonstrate the number and malice of the spirits and to give the man unambiguous evidence of his deliverance. Bede adds that the pigs rushing into the lake is a figure of the self-destructive nature of demonic influence when unleashed from a human host.

The townspeople's fear at seeing the demoniac "sitting there, dressed and in his mind" is, for Chrysostom, a profound comment on human perversity: they are more comfortable with a known disorder than an unexpected healing. The request that Jesus leave their region is, for Origen, a type of those who love their property more than the presence of God. The man's request to accompany Jesus is refused — he is instead sent back as a missionary to his own people. Chrysostom notes that he is the first Gentile missionary in Mark, commissioned not from Jerusalem but from Decapolis.

Legion — for we are many. The plural within the singular (“my name is Legion / for we are many”) reflects demonic fragmentation: the possessed man has no single self left. The restoration in v.15 — sitting, clothed, in his right mind — is the reversal of every element of the possession.

Go home to your own people — the first explicit mission. Jesus breaks the Messianic Secret here because this is Gentile territory where there is no messianic expectation to be managed. The restored man becomes Mark’s first evangelist.

Calvin: The naming of the demons as Legion is not merely descriptive but a confession of defeat. When demons name themselves to Jesus they are acknowledging his authority to command them. The name is given in response to Jesus’s question — he has power to compel even the identification of his enemies.
The mission to the Decapolis is the first deliberate Gentile outreach. Calvin notes that Jesus employs a man who is not one of the Twelve — the Spirit’s mission is not confined to official channels.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The herd rushed down the bank into the lake — the verb hormēsen (rushed/charged) is violent and directional. The demons’ destination is the sea, which in apocalyptic tradition is the domain of chaos and the Abyss. Their request to enter the pigs rather than be sent to the Abyss (v.10) is ultimately futile — they end in the sea anyway.

He began to tell in the Decapolis — the ten Greek cities of the Transjordan region. The Gentile mission, barely visible in Mark, is planted here. By the time of Acts 9 there are disciples throughout the region.

hist.context: The Gerasene exorcism is the longest and most dramatic in Mark. Three elements make it distinctive: the man’s extreme condition (tombs, chains, self-harm), the demons’ naming as Legion, and the first explicit mission to Gentiles — Jesus tells the restored man to go home and testify in the Decapolis, breaking the Messianic Secret pattern that governs Jewish territory.

sections.header: Verses 21–34 — Jairus’s Plea; The Woman Who Touched His Cloak

heb.paragraph: Talitha koum is one of the few places where Mark preserves Jesus’s actual Aramaic words, untranslated — then immediately translates them. The preservation of the original Aramaic is a mark of eyewitness transmission. Peter would have remembered the exact words in the room.

places.name: Region of the Gerasenes
places.coords: Eastern shore of the Sea of Galilee; Decapolis territory
places.text: This is Gentile land — the pigs confirm it. The Decapolis (ten Greek cities) was a region of Hellenistic culture east of the Jordan. Jesus’s first extended Gentile engagement happens at the far edge of Jewish geography, in a cemetery, among unclean animals.

places.name: The Sea / Steep Bank
places.text: The herd of 2,000 pigs rushing down the steep bank into the sea dramatizes the exorcism’s scale and irrevocability. The sea, which Jesus crossed in the storm, now becomes the grave of Legion.

refs.note: A woman with an abnormal discharge — the Levitical law that the bleeding woman has been under. She has been ceremonially unclean for twelve years, excluded from worship and touch.
Elijah raises the widow’s son — the closest OT parallel to a resurrection. Jesus does what the prophet did, but by command rather than prayer.
You brought me up from the realm of the dead — the psalm of resurrection that frames the raising of Jairus’s daughter.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: The woman's twelve-year hemorrhage made her ritually impure under Lev 15:25-30 — she would have contaminated anyone she touched. Her touching Jesus's cloak is an act of ritual transgression driven by desperation. Marcus notes that Jesus does not rebuke her for the transgression but commends her faith. The legal contamination-logic is simply overridden: in Jesus's presence the flow of uncleanness is reversed to a flow of power (dynamis, v.30).

Marcus: "Don't be afraid; just believe" — Marcus reads this as a summary of Mark's entire theology of faith. In Mark's narrative, faith is not intellectual assent but the act of coming to Jesus despite every obstacle (the woman pushing through the crowd; Jairus maintaining hope after hearing his daughter is dead). Marcus connects this to the Habakkuk pesher at Qumran (1QpHab 8:1-3), where "the righteous lives by faith" is interpreted as trusting the Teacher of Righteousness despite apparent delay of fulfilment.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The Jairus / bleeding woman sandwich is the most fully developed intercalation in Mark. The structural principle: the two stories interpret each other. The woman's twelve-year illness and the girl's twelve years of life are numerically linked. The woman's healing "on the way" creates the time-delay that allows Jairus's daughter to die. Faith is tested in the interval (5:35-36) and rewarded by resurrection. Rhoads argues that Mark's intercalation is not merely a compositional device but a theological statement: interruptions do not cancel God's action — they create the space in which faith is deepened.

Rhoads: "Talitha koum" — the Aramaic preserved in the narrator's voice is one of several places where Mark includes an untranslated saying and then translates it. The effect is to give the reader access to two layers: the reported original voice of Jesus in his mother tongue, and the narrator's explanation for non-Aramaic readers. This dual layer implies an audience distant from the original setting — confirming the Gospel's self-presentation as translation of an eyewitness tradition (cf. Papias on Mark as Peter's interpreter).

The interlocking of Jairus's daughter and the bleeding woman is, for Ambrose, deliberate literary theology: the interruption is not a delay but a demonstration. Both sufferers are female; one is twelve years old, the other has been ill twelve years — Bede and Theophylact both note this parallel. The woman's touch of the hem of Jesus's garment is, for Chrysostom, a picture of faith that needs no formal request — she does not ask, she reaches, and power goes out. Jesus's question "Who touched me?" is not ignorance but invitation: he wishes to make her healing public and complete.

The raising of Jairus's daughter begins with the most devastating news imaginable — "your daughter is dead" — and Jesus's response is, for Chrysostom, the paradigm of faith under extreme testing: "Don't be afraid; just believe." The exclusion of the mourners and the admission of only three disciples and the parents is, for Origen, a principle of spiritual economy — the mystery of resurrection is not for public exhibition but for intimate witness. The command "Talitha koum" is preserved in Aramaic by Mark alone, which Bede says is because it carries its power in the original sound.

Your faith has healed you — the woman’s touch was an act of desperate, theologically imperfect faith. She hoped for a quasi-magical transfer of power. Jesus does not correct her theology in public but affirms the faith and converts the transaction into a covenant encounter: “Daughter, go in peace.”
Talitha koum — the Aramaic command is preserved because Peter was in the room. The intimacy of the small word — “little girl” — is as characteristic of Jesus as the power behind it.

Don’t be afraid; just believe — the two-word command is the Gospel in miniature. Fear and faith are mutually exclusive postures. Jairus is at the exact moment where faith either grows or collapses under the weight of news. Jesus’s command does not deny the news; it relocates its significance.

He told them to give her something to eat — the practical concern after the miracle. Calvin: Jesus demonstrates that the raised girl is genuinely alive, not a ghost or vision. The request for food is theological: the resurrection is bodily, not merely spiritual.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: She had suffered a great deal under the care of many doctors — a realistic detail that was softened in Luke’s parallel (Luke 8:43, which omits the doctors’ failure). Mark’s version preserves the social reality: twelve years of medical failure and financial ruin.

He gave strict orders not to let anyone know — the Messianic Secret applied to a resurrection. The crowds already know the girl was dead (v.40: they laughed at Jesus). The command is not to suppress the resurrection but to control the interpretation of who Jesus is.

hist.context: Mark interweaves two restoration stories — the technique known as a Markan sandwich. Jairus’s dying daughter provides the frame; the bleeding woman is inserted into the middle. The structural connection is the number twelve: the woman bled for twelve years; the girl is twelve years old. Both are daughters (v.23, v.34), both are restored to full life.

sections.header: Verses 35–43 — Jairus’s Daughter Raised: Talitha Koum

heb.paragraph: My name is Legion — a Roman military unit of 3,000–6,000 soldiers. The name is both descriptive (many demons) and politically charged: Rome’s military power and demonic oppression use the same vocabulary. The exorcism in Gentile territory that destroys the pigs reads, for Mark’s Roman audience, as a cosmic challenge to Roman imperial power.

Three elements make it distinctive: the man’s extreme condition (tombs, chains, self-harm), the demons’ naming as Legion, and the first explicit mission to Gentiles — Jesus tells the restored man to go home and testify in the Decapolis, breaking the Messianic Secret pattern that governs Jewish territory.

ppl.name: Jairus
ppl.role: Synagogue leader; father of the raised girl
ppl.text: His public prostration before Jesus (v.22) represents an extraordinary act of submission from a communal authority. His faith is tested to the limit by the news of his daughter’s death.

ppl.name: The Gerasene demoniac
ppl.text: Restored from extreme possession to clothed sanity. Jesus’s first explicit commission: go and tell. He proclaims throughout the Decapolis, planting the first Gentile witness.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Talitha koum!" (which means "Little girl, I say to you, get up!")ESV"Talitha cumi," which means, "Little girl, I say to you, arise."KJV"Talitha cumi; which is, being interpreted, Damsel, I say unto thee, arise."NASB"Talitha kum!" (which translated means, "Little girl, I say to you, get up!")NLT"Talitha koum," which means "Little girl, get up!"

src.title: Babylonian Talmud, Sanhedrin 17a
src.quote: Rabbinic discussion of resurrection expectations in Second Temple Judaism.
src.note: Provides the theological frame within which Jesus’s raising of Jairus’s daughter would have been heard.

src.title: Josephus, Jewish War 3.446
src.quote: Josephus describes the Decapolis as a region of Greek cities east of the Jordan — confirming the Gentile character of the Gerasene territory in Mark 5.
src.note: Confirms the geographical and cultural context of Jesus’s Gentile mission in Mark 5.

rec.text: Augustine used the two stories (5:21-43) to argue that faith in Jesus overcomes both chronic suffering (twelve years) and death. The woman represents those whose faith is small and grasping; Jairus represents those whose faith is tested at the extreme.

Barth identified the Jairus story as the paradigm of “the awakening of the dead” — the eschatological pattern made visible in a specific historical act. Every resurrection follows the Talitha koum pattern: a word that overcomes death.

rows.label: The Markan Sandwich: Twelve and Twelve

hebtext.note: My name is Legion — a Roman military unit of 3,000–6,000 soldiers. The name is both descriptive (many demons) and politically charged: Rome’s military power and demonic oppression use the same vocabulary. The exorcism in Gentile territory that destroys the pigs reads, for Mark’s Roman audience, as a cosmic challenge to Roman imperial power.

Talitha koum is one of the few places where Mark preserves Jesus’s actual Aramaic words, untranslated — then immediately translates them. The preservation of the original Aramaic is a mark of eyewitness transmission. Peter would have remembered the exact words in the room.

The name is both descriptive (many demons) and politically charged: Rome’s military power and demonic oppression use the same vocabulary. The exorcism in Gentile territory that destroys the pigs reads, for Mark’s Roman audience, as a cosmic challenge to Roman imperial power.

tx.title: Gerasenes, Gadarenes, or Gergesenes
tx.content: Sinaiticus, Alexandrinusread Gerasēnōn;other MSSGadarēnōn (Matthew's parallel) or Gergesēnōn (Origen's preferred reading based on topography).
tx.note: Gerasa is 37km from the lake — geographically problematic. Gadara is closer but still several kilometres. "Gergesenes" (Origen) may be a scholarly correction. No reading is free of difficulty; Gerasenes is the best-attested.

tx.title: "Overhearing" or "ignoring"
tx.note: Parakoysas is the harder reading (ambiguous: overhear or disregard) and likely original; it fits the urgency of the scene.

debate.title: Did Jesus raise the dead or was she sleeping?

positions.name: Resurrection view
positions.proponents: Jesus’s statement “she is not dead but asleep” is a metaphor for resurrection, not a medical diagnosis. The crowd knew she was dead (v.35). Sleeping is Jesus’s frame for what death looks like from his perspective.

positions.name: Resuscitation view
positions.proponents: Some suggest the girl was in a deep coma and Jesus correctly diagnosed it. The crowd’s laughter (v.40) supports the possibility that her death was uncertain.

themes.note: Mark 5 is the authority chapter par excellence: three demonstrations of Jesus's power over demonic forces, chronic disease, and death itself. Each restoration is more extreme than the last.

coaching.tip: The Gerasene demoniac: Legion-possessed, living among tombs, uncontrollable. Jesus casts demons into pigs; the townspeople ask him to leave. They preferred the familiar chaos to transforming power.
Jairus's daughter and the hemorrhaging woman—two intertwined healings. Twelve years of bleeding; a twelve-year-old girl. One touches Jesus secretly; one Jesus raises publicly. Faith connects both.
